questions.q: Separation of powers divides government functions among how many main branches?
questions.explanation: The doctrine divides government among three branches: the legislature, the executive and the judiciary.

questions.q: Which branch is primarily responsible for making laws?
questions.explanation: The legislature is the law-making branch of government.

questions.q: Which branch is primarily responsible for implementing and enforcing laws?
questions.explanation: The executive branch implements and enforces the laws.

questions.q: Which branch is primarily responsible for interpreting laws and settling disputes?
questions.explanation: The judiciary interprets laws and adjudicates disputes.

questions.q: Separation of powers is regarded as part of which feature of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Separation of powers is regarded as part of the basic structure of the Constitution.

questions.q: The system by which each branch can limit the others is commonly called what?
questions.explanation: Checks and balances allow each branch to limit the powers of the others.

questions.q: In India, the Parliament of India belongs to which branch of government?
questions.explanation: The Parliament of India is the legislative branch at the Union level.

questions.q: The Supreme Court of India belongs to which branch of government?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court is the apex body of the judicial branch.

questions.q: The Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister forms part of which branch?
questions.explanation: The Council of Ministers is part of the executive branch.

questions.q: The power of courts to examine the validity of laws and executive actions is known as what?
questions.explanation: Judicial review allows courts to test the constitutional validity of laws and executive actions, a key check among the branches.

questions.q: Which Article of the Constitution provides that the executive power of the Union is vested in the President?
questions.explanation: Article 53 vests the executive power of the Union in the President.

questions.q: Which Article provides for a Council of Ministers to aid and advise the President?
questions.explanation: Article 74 provides for a Council of Ministers to aid and advise the President in exercising functions.

questions.q: Which Article seeks to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State?
questions.explanation: Article 50, a Directive Principle, directs the State to separate the judiciary from the executive.

questions.q: Which Article establishes Parliament, consisting of the President and the two Houses?
questions.explanation: Article 79 constitutes Parliament, comprising the President and the two Houses.

questions.q: Articles 121 and 211 place restrictions on legislatures discussing the conduct of whom in the discharge of their duties?
questions.explanation: Articles 121 and 211 restrict discussion in legislatures on the conduct of judges, protecting judicial independence.

questions.q: In the 1973 Kesavananda Bharati case, separation of powers was recognised as part of what?
options: The basic structure of the Constitution
questions.explanation: The Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) recognised separation of powers as part of the Constitution's basic structure.

questions.q: The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to which House, illustrating the link between executive and legislature?
questions.explanation: Under Article 75, the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.

questions.q: India follows which model with respect to a strict separation of powers?
options: A flexible separation with overlapping functions, not a rigid one
Separation only at the state level
questions.explanation: India does not follow a rigid separation; the branches overlap with checks and balances under a parliamentary system.

questions.q: The power of the President to promulgate ordinances under Article 123 is an example of the executive exercising what kind of function?
options: A legislative function when Parliament is not in session
questions.explanation: Ordinance-making under Article 123 lets the executive perform a legislative function when Parliament is not in session, an overlap of powers.

questions.q: Parliament's power to remove a judge of the Supreme Court through an address is an example of which branch checking another?
questions.explanation: The removal of a judge through a parliamentary address is a legislative check on the judiciary.

questions.q: When a legislature itself acts as a court in adjudicating a breach of its privileges, this reflects which overlap?
options: The legislature exercising a quasi-judicial function
The judiciary exercising a legislative function
The executive exercising a judicial function
questions.explanation: Adjudicating breaches of privilege shows the legislature performing a quasi-judicial function, an overlap of roles.

questions.q: The classical theory of separation of powers is most associated with which 18th-century thinker?
questions.explanation: The doctrine of separation of powers is classically associated with the French thinker Montesquieu.

questions.q: In which 1967 case did the Supreme Court hold that Parliament could not amend fundamental rights, before the basic structure doctrine was settled?
options: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
questions.explanation: In Golak Nath (1967) the Court limited Parliament's amending power, part of the evolution toward the basic structure doctrine.

questions.q: In the 1975 Indira Nehru Gandhi election case, the Supreme Court struck down a provision partly because it violated which principle?
options: Separation of powers and judicial review
questions.explanation: The 1975 election case reinforced separation of powers and judicial review as part of the basic structure.

questions.q: Which Article enables the President to seek the advisory opinion of the Supreme Court, an interaction between the executive and judiciary?
questions.explanation: Article 143 allows the President to refer questions to the Supreme Court for its advisory opinion.

questions.q: The Constitution vests the legislative, executive and judicial powers of the State in distinct organs, but the executive's general power is co-extensive with whose legislative power?
options: The legislature's, in respect of subjects it can legislate on
questions.explanation: The executive power of the Union or a State is generally co-extensive with the legislative power of Parliament or the state legislature.

questions.q: The pardoning power of the President under Article 72 is an example of the executive exercising a power that overlaps with which branch's domain?
questions.explanation: The pardoning power lets the executive modify the effect of a judicial sentence, an overlap with the judicial domain.

questions.q: Which provision protects members of Parliament from court proceedings for anything said or any vote given in Parliament, safeguarding legislative independence?
questions.explanation: Article 105 grants members of Parliament immunity from court proceedings for speech and votes in the House.

questions.q: Which Article makes laws declared by the Supreme Court binding on all courts within India, reflecting the judiciary's authority within the constitutional scheme?
questions.explanation: Article 141 provides that the law declared by the Supreme Court is binding on all courts in India.

questions.q: Article 122 limits the power of courts to inquire into proceedings of Parliament on the ground of what?
questions.explanation: Article 122 bars courts from inquiring into parliamentary proceedings on grounds of procedural irregularity, protecting legislative autonomy.
